title: The Trigger in Stillness
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: For those with complex trauma, stillness can feel like a threat. Discover why quiet moments can trigger your nervous system and how to find safety from within.
imageDescription: A warm, luminous light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, repaired cracks of a kintsugi bowl. The golden lines trace paths across the ceramic surface, suggesting that healing is not about erasing the damage, but about transforming it into something beautiful and strong. The scene is quiet, contemplative, and filled with a sense of gentle resilience.
htmlContent:
Jiddu Krishnamurti spent a lifetime dismantling the architecture of the self, suggesting that true freedom arises from observation without an observer, a state of pure awareness where the division between the thinker and the thought dissolves. This is not a state to be achieved through striving, but a returning to something fundamental that was never lost, only obscured by the noise of our own conditioning. For those of us who carry the intricate weight of complex trauma, the very idea of stillness can feel less like a sanctuary and more like a threat—a silence filled not with peace, but with the echoes of what the body has been forced to remember. The quiet moments can become the very landscapes where the past erupts into the present, not as a memory, but as a full-body experience.

The Paradox of Quiet: When Stillness Activates the Threat Response

For a nervous system wired by developmental trauma, the absence of external chaos can paradoxically trigger an internal one. We spend so much of our lives in a state of hypervigilance that our entire physiology becomes organized around mobilization—the fight, flight, or freeze responses that ensured our survival. What happens, then, when the external environment finally becomes safe, but the body’s alarm system is still ringing with an intensity that bears no relationship to the present moment? This is the cruel paradox of the trigger in stillness: the moment you stop running, the tiger you were running from seems to finally catch up. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve sat with countless individuals who describe this phenomenon as a profound betrayal by their own bodies.

The architecture of this response is rooted deep in our neurobiology, a concept that the work of researchers like Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory has helped to illuminate. The nervous system is constantly asking an implicit question: “Am I safe?” This process, which Porges calls neuroception, happens far below conscious thought. When we are in an environment that lacks the cues of safety—not just the absence of threat, but the active presence of connection and co-regulation—the system can default to its oldest survival states. Stillness, for a system that learned that quiet was often the precursor to harm, can be interpreted by the body as a profound and terrifying form of abandonment or a signal that danger is imminent.

The research is clear on this, and it contradicts almost everything popular culture teaches.

We are told to meditate or to simply be with ourselves, but for many, this is the equivalent of being locked in a room with the very thing they have spent a lifetime trying to outrun. The work, then, is not to force stillness upon a system that perceives it as a threat, but to begin a slow and patient process of re-negotiating the meaning of quiet, of titrating moments of peace, and of discovering what it means to be with oneself without being consumed by the past.

Deconstructing the Inner Critic: The Voice That Fills the Silence

When the external world goes quiet, another voice often rushes in to fill the void—the relentless, punishing voice of the inner critic. This is not merely negative self-talk; for survivors of complex trauma, it is often an internalized echo of the abusers or environments that taught them that they were fundamentally flawed. This critical part of us, as explained through frameworks like Internal Family Systems (IFS), is not an enemy to be vanquished but a protective part that took on a difficult job in an attempt to keep us safe. It learned that if it could criticize us first, it might be able to prevent the external punishment or abandonment that felt so life-threatening.

In the stillness, this protector part can become incredibly loud, its litany of perceived failures echoing in the chamber of a quiet mind. It tells us we are not doing enough, not healing fast enough, not being productive enough. This is not a malfunction; it is the protector’s desperate attempt to manage an internal state of anxiety and to prevent a repeat of past relational ruptures. The tragedy is that the very strategy it uses to keep us safe is the one that perpetuates the core wound of unworthiness. A client once described this to me as having a permanent heckler in the audience of her life, one who knew all of her deepest fears and wasn't afraid to shout them from the back row.

The path forward does not involve arguing with this critic or trying to silence it by force. Instead, it involves a gentle and courageous turning toward it, an acknowledgment of its protective intent, however misguided its methods may be. It is the work of separating the voice of the critic from the truth of our own being, of recognizing that this is a part of us, not the whole of us. It is, as Krishnamurti might suggest, the act of observing the critic without becoming the critic, of witnessing the storm of self-recrimination without being swept away by it. What happens when we can create even a sliver of space between our awareness and that critical voice?

The Tyranny of Productivity: When Doing Becomes a Defense Against Being

For many of us who have navigated complex trauma, staying busy is not a lifestyle choice; it is a survival strategy. We learn to fill every available moment with tasks, with work, with anything that keeps us in motion and prevents us from having to feel the disquiet that hums just beneath the surface. Productivity becomes a defense mechanism, a way to prove our worth and a powerful distraction from the internal landscape of unresolved grief, anger, and fear. The relentless pursuit of accomplishment can feel like a forward momentum, but it is often a highly sophisticated form of avoidance, a way of running from the stillness where the un-felt feelings reside.

This addiction to doing is not a personal failing but a deeply ingrained adaptation of the nervous system. When we are in a state of sympathetic activation—the “fight or flight” response—our bodies are flooded with stress hormones, creating a physiological imperative to act. In a chronically unsafe environment, this state can become a baseline, a way of being that feels normal even long after the danger has passed. The thought of stopping, of resting, of simply being, can therefore feel profoundly dysregulating, signaling to the body that we are letting our guard down. The stillness, in this context, can feel like a kind of death.

Consciousness doesn't arrive. It's what's left when everything else quiets down.

The work here is not to condemn our own busyness but to inquire into its origins with curiosity. It is to begin to notice the subtle impulse to check our phone or start another task the moment a pocket of unstructured time appears. This is not about forcing ourselves into long periods of meditation. It is about starting small, with micro-doses of stillness—the thirty seconds while the kettle boils, the two minutes sitting in the car after we’ve parked. It is in these small, intentional moments of non-doing that we begin to build the capacity to be with ourselves and to discover that we can, in fact, survive the quiet.

Embodied Awareness: The Body as an Anchor in the Storm

So much of the conversation around healing is centered in the mind—in changing our thoughts and understanding our patterns. While this cognitive work has its place, it often fails to touch the core of the traumatic wound, which is held not in the story we tell, but in the sensory and physiological residue that lives on in our bodies. We cannot think our way out of a dysregulated nervous system. The path back to a sense of wholeness is not through the intellect alone, but through the direct, felt experience of the body. This is the essence of embodiment—the practice of inhabiting our physical selves and discovering the body not as a source of betrayal, but as a wise companion.

For those of us who have experienced trauma, the body can feel like a dangerous place. We may have learned to dissociate or live in our heads as a way of surviving unbearable sensations. Reconnecting with the body, therefore, must be a slow, respectful, and consent-based process. It is not about forcing ourselves to feel everything at once, but about gently re-establishing a connection with our physical experience. This might begin with something as simple as noticing the feeling of our feet on the floor, or the sensation of our breath. These are small anchors in the present moment, gentle tethers that can pull our awareness out of the swirling vortex of past and future and into the immediacy of right now.

Embodiment is not a technique. It's what happens when you stop living exclusively in your head.

Practices like Somatic Experiencing, developed by Peter Levine, are built on this principle of titrating our experience, of touching into discomfort just enough to allow the body to process and release stored survival energy, and then returning to a place of resource. It is the work of pendulation, of moving between the activation of the trauma and the calm of the present moment, slowly expanding our window of tolerance. This is not about re-living the trauma, but about allowing the body to complete the defensive responses that were interrupted. It is in this completion that the nervous system can finally begin to settle and come to rest in the safety of the present.

The Architecture of Returning: Building Scaffolding for Safety

If stillness has become a trigger, then the journey of healing is not about demolishing our defenses but about building a new kind of internal architecture—one that can support a growing sense of safety from the inside out. This is not a passive process of waiting for the world to feel safe, but an active and creative one of building the scaffolding that allows our nervous system to begin to experience safety. This scaffolding is constructed from the deliberate and consistent practice of turning toward ourselves with a quality of attention that is both kind and unwavering. It is the work of becoming the safe attachment figure for the parts of ourselves that have felt abandoned and alone.

This process involves a radical re-orientation of our relationship with ourselves. It means learning to track our own nervous system state with a non-judgmental awareness, noticing the subtle shifts from a state of connection to one of activation or shutdown. It means cultivating a rich internal and external resource network—the people, places, and practices that help our system regulate. This could be the warmth of a cup of tea, the sound of music, or a brief call with a trusted friend. These are not indulgences; they are essential tools for nervous system regulation, the building blocks of our internal scaffolding.

As we begin to build this internal structure, we can start to experiment with stillness in a way that feels manageable. We can create “islands of quiet” in our busy lives—small, contained moments where we intentionally pause and turn our attention inward, knowing that we have the resources to navigate whatever we might find there. This is a stillness that is earned, built on a foundation of self-trust and self-compassion. It is a stillness that is not empty, but full of the quiet, steady presence of our own awareness. It is a returning to the truth that Jiddu Krishnamurti pointed to: that in this unified field of awareness, there is a profound and unshakable peace that has been here all along.

Are you willing to be uncomfortable for longer than feels reasonable? Because that is what this asks. Not permanent discomfort. But the willingness to stay in the difficult space long enough for something to shift. Most people leave too early. The question is whether you will.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my anxiety get worse when I try to relax?

When your nervous system has been conditioned to equate relaxation with danger, letting your guard down can trigger a powerful threat response. Your body is sounding an alarm, believing that the absence of tension is a sign that you are unsafe. The work is to gently teach your system that it is possible to be relaxed and safe at the same time.

What if I can't identify any specific traumatic memories?

Complex trauma is often not about a single event, but about a chronic lack of safety and attunement during key developmental periods. The body remembers this on a physiological level, even if there are no explicit narrative memories. You do not need a clear story to begin the work of healing the underlying nervous system dysregulation.

How can I tell the difference between my intuition and a trauma response?

A trauma response often feels like a loud, urgent, and catastrophic alarm bell. Intuition, on the other hand, tends to be quieter, clearer, and more grounded. Learning to differentiate the two involves slowing down, connecting with the body, and noticing the specific sensory and emotional textures of each experience.

Is it possible to ever feel truly safe in my own body?

Yes, it is possible, though the path is not linear. Feeling safe in your own body is not a final destination, but an ongoing practice of returning, moment by moment, to a state of embodied presence and self-compassion. It involves grieving the safety you never had and building new neural pathways of regulation and connection.

I feel like I'm just going in circles. How do I know if I'm making progress?

Progress in trauma healing is rarely linear; it often looks more like a spiral. Instead of looking for a constant upward trajectory, look for the subtle shifts, like catching a trigger sooner or returning to regulation more quickly. Maybe you are able to set a boundary you couldn't have set a year ago. These are the real markers of progress on the healing path. The healing journey is not a straight line.

The stillness does not have to be the enemy. It can, with time and patient practice, become the very ground from which a new and more integrated way of being can emerge. What would it be to allow the quiet, just for a moment, to hold you rather than haunt you?
